content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 26, 1989

Ms. Ellen Fletcher
777-108 San Antonio Road
Palo Alto, CA 94303

RE: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. I-89-231

Dear Ms. Fletcher:

You have requested advice concerning the campaign disclosure
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1/ Because you
have not asked a specific question of interpretation under the
Act, we treat your letter as a request for informal assistance
pursuant to Regulation 18239(c).2/

You have asked what your options are for the use of surplus
campaign funds remaining in your campaign account. You do not
intend to seek re-election.

The Political Reform Act, as amended by Proposition 73,
states that campaign funds shall be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the candidate to a
specific office or expenses associated with holding that office.
(Section 85202.) In addition, Section 85304 prohibits a candidate
for elective office from transferring funds to another candidate.

California Elections Code Section 12400 et seq. prohibits the
personal use of campaign funds and sets out the permissible use of
surplus campaign funds. You may want to contact Ted Prim with

_______________________
1/ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2/ Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3).)

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660

Ellen Fletcher
Page 2


the State Attorney General's office at 916/324-5481 for an
interpretation of the Elections Code Section 12400.

If you have specific questions that are not addressed in this
letter, please write to me.

Sincerely,

Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel

[illegible]

By:  Mary Ann Kvasager
Political Reform Consultant

FPPC
APR 14 1:54 PM '89

April 12, 1989

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, #800
Sacramento, CA 94814

Subject: Use of campaign funds

Dear FPPC:

I am a City Councilmember. My term expires at the end of 1989 and I still have a campaign fund in an interest-bearing account. I do not intend to run for re-election in the coming election.

What are my options for use of the money in the campaign fund?

Sincerely,

Ellen Fletcher
Ellen Fletcher

777-108 San Antonio Road
Palo Alto, CA 94303
415/494-8943

894-231

FPPC
Apr 14 | 54 PM '09

April 12, 1989

Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, #800
Sacramento, CA 94814

Subject: Use of campaign funds

Dear FPPC:

I am a City Councilmember. My term expires at the end of 1989 and I still
have a campaign fund in an interest-bearing account. I do not intend to run
for re-election in the coming election.

What are my options for use of the money in the campaign fund?

Sincerely,

Ellen Fletcher
Ellen Fletcher

777-108 San Antonio Road
Palo Alto, CA 94303
415/494-8943

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 19, 1989


Ellen Fletcher
777-108 San Antonio Road
Palo Alto, CA  94303

Re:  Letter No. 89-231

Dear Ms. Fletcher:

     Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform Act
was received on April 17, 1989 by the Fair Political Practices
Commission. If you have any questions about your advice request,
you may contact me directly at (916) 322-5662.

     We try to answer all advice requests promptly.  Therefore,
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, or
more information is needed, you should expect a response within 21
working days if your request seeks formal written advice.  If more
information is needed, the person assigned to prepare a response
to your request will contact you shortly to advise you as to the
information needed.  If your request is for informal assistance,
we will answer it as quickly as we can.  (See Commission
Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 18329).)

     You also should be aware that your letter and our response
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon
receipt of a proper request for disclosure.

Very truly yours,

[signature]
Jeanne Pritchard
Chief Technical Assistance
and Analysis Division

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

sections.question: You have asked what your options are for the use of surplus campaign funds remaining in your campaign account. You do not intend to seek re-election.
sections.conclusion: The Political Reform Act, as amended by Proposition 73, states that campaign funds shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate to a specific office or expenses associated with holding that office. (Section 85202.) In addition, section 85304 prohibits a candidate for elective office from transferring funds to another candidate. California Elections Code section 12400 et seq. prohibits the personal use of campaign funds and sets out the permissible use of surplus campaign funds. You may want to contact Ted Prim with the state Attorney General's office at 916/324-5481 for an interpretation of the Elections Code section 12400.
sections.question_synthetic: What are the permissible uses for surplus campaign funds that remain in a campaign account when the candidate does not intend to seek re-election?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC notes that campaign funds are held in trust for election and holding office expenses under the Political Reform Act and Proposition 73, and that Elections Code section 12400 et seq. governs the permissible uses of surplus funds. The FPPC refers the requestor to the state Attorney General's office for specific guidance on surplus campaign fund disposition under Elections Code section 12400.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both the original incoming request (April 19, 1989 acknowledgment letter) and the FPPC informal advice response (September 26, 1989). Treating this as the FPPC response letter. The response letter explicitly states it is informal assistance pursuant to Regulation 18239(c), not a formal opinion. No explicit CONCLUSION or SHORT ANSWER section heading, but substantive conclusion provided in body of letter.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You have asked what your options are for the use of surplus campaign funds remaining in your campaign account. You do not intend to seek re-election.

CONCLUSION: The Political Reform Act, as amended by Proposition 73, states that campaign funds shall be deemed to be held in trust for expenses associated with the election of the candidate to a specific office or expenses associated with holding that office. (Section 85202.) In addition, section 85304 prohibits a candidate for elective office from transferring funds to another candidate. California Elections Code section 12400 et seq. prohibits the personal use of campaign funds and sets out the permissible use of surplus campaign funds. You may want to contact Ted Prim with the state Attorney General's office at 916/324-5481 for an interpretation of the Elections Code section 12400.

embedding.summary: Ms. Ellen Fletcher, a term-limited board member, requested informal advice on options for using surplus campaign funds she does not intend to use since she will not seek re-election. The FPPC responded that the Political Reform Act treats campaign funds as held in trust for election and office-related expenses, directing her to the Attorney General's office for specific guidance on surplus fund uses under Elections Code section 12400.
